learning_outcomes.description: Students will develop advanced translation skills from ancient Greek into English by reading a wider range of unadapted ancient Greek poetry.
Students will develop advanced skills in the critical evaluation of ancient Greek literature, using their developing linguistic expertise to open up larger thematic and historical issues.

Students will develop a deep understanding of the oldest and most influential of ancient Greek literary genres, including not only its peculiar dialect, metre, and habits of expression, but also its larger habits of thought.
Students will develop an understanding of the political, social, and cultural context of the production, dissemination, and reception of Homeric poetry.
Students will develop the ability to evaluate ethically issues and questions raised by the text(s) pertaining to freedom, violence, and justice.

Students will have the opportunity to demonstrate transferable workplace skills through class activities and assessment tasks which develop advanced written and verbal communication tasks, advanced problem solving, leadership, and critical thinking skills.

assessments.task_description:
Mid-Semester exam will be held in person in class.

The mid-semester exam requires students to:

·       translate from Greek into English passages from the text(s) set for analysis in Weeks 1-6

·       answer parsing, grammatical, and literary analysis questions on the set passages

·       translate a short, unseen passage from elsewhere in the Iliad. Words that do not appear in the lines covered in Weeks 1-6 will be glossed.

 

Further details will be provided on Blackboard. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course


Unacknowledged use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment or unapproved learning tasks may be referred for academic misconduct.

Use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment tasks while language learning skills are still being developed is likely to result in students failing to meet core learning outcomes for the course. Students relying on Machine Translation and AI to complete assessment are also more likely to fail in-person assessment tasks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This exam will be undertaken in class, in person, and handed in to the course coordinator at the end of the exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
27/10/2026 2:00 pm

Submit via turnitin as instructed on Blackboard
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to write a critical evaluation essay based on the text(s) set for analysis. Students are also required to provide their own English translations of the Greek text(s).

 This essay requires you to demonstrate your understanding of the Greek text(s) set for analysis. It does not require extensive reading of modern sources outside the recommended reading list on Blackboard, unless you come across something relevant which you use critically in your analysis.

 

Please Note: A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria.

 Essay Questions and further details will be made available on Blackboard, and students will have the opportunity to practice and discuss the type of analysis required in class before the submission date.

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

Unacknowledged use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment or unapproved learning tasks may be referred for academic misconduct.

Use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment tasks while language learning skills are still being developed is likely to result in students failing to meet core learning outcomes for the course. Students relying on Machine Translation and AI to complete assessment are also more likely to fail in-person assessment tasks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit through the Turn-It-In link on Blackboard
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The Final Exam for GREK3002 is an in-person exam scheduled in the exam period by UQ Examinations


The Final Exam requires students to :

  • translate from Greek into English a passage from the text(s) set for analysis in Weeks 7-13
  • scan two lines of the set passage comment on features of thematic and stylistic interest within the passage, in the context of the Iliad as a whole 
  • translate a short, unseen passage from elsewhere in the Iliad. Words that do not appear in Book 1 or Book 24 will be glossed.


Further details will be provided on Blackboard.

 Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course


Unacknowledged use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment or unapproved learning tasks may be referred for academic misconduct.

Use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment tasks while language learning skills are still being developed is likely to result in students failing to meet core learning outcomes for the course. Students relying on Machine Translation and AI to complete assessment are also more likely to fail in-person assessment tasks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
• Each week, students will be expected to prepare about 100 lines of Greek, and to bring three (at least!) queries about the language they have encountered. They must participate actively in group discussion of the text and its meaning.
  •  Each student will also be assigned a number of lines within that 100-line section, which they must be ready to present on, both translating for the class and leading a short discussion.
  •  Students will also be required to do a 5 min presentation on a scholarly publication on the Iliad.

 

Further information will be provided on Blackboard and in class. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course

Unacknowledged use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment or unapproved learning tasks may be referred for academic misconduct.

Use of Machine Translation and/or AI to complete assessment tasks while language learning skills are still being developed is likely to result in students failing to meet core learning outcomes for the course. Students relying on Machine Translation and AI to complete assessment are also more likely to fail in-person assessment tasks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In person translation, discussion, and presentation during class time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
